content:
First, observe that if any digit of \(n\) is 0 then the product of the digits is 0, so we would need
\[
n^2-15n-27=0,
\]
whose roots are \(\tfrac{15\pm\sqrt{333}}2\), not integers.  Hence all digits of \(n\) are non‐zero and the product of the digits is at least 1, so
\[
n^2-15n-27\ge1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
n^2-15n-28\ge0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
n\ge17\quad(\text{or }n\le-2),
\]
and we only check positive \(n\ge1\).  On the other hand, for a \(d\)‐digit number the product of its digits is at most \(9^d\), while
\[
n^2-15n-27\sim n^2
\]
grows much faster, so in fact one shows there are no solutions once \(n\ge37\).  Thus we only need to test \(1\le n\le36\), and a quick check finds the lone solution
\[
n=17,\quad\text{since }1\cdot7=7
\quad\text{and}\quad
17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7.
\]
No other \(n\) in \([1,36]\) works.  Hence the sum of all solutions is
\[
\boxed{17}.
\]

First, observe that if any digit of \(n\) is 0 then the product of the digits is 0, so we would need
\[
n^2-15n-27=0,
\]
whose roots are \(\tfrac{15\pm\sqrt{333}}2\), not integers.  Hence all digits of \(n\) are non‐zero and the product of the digits is at least 1, so
\[
n^2-15n-27\ge1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
n^2-15n-28\ge0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
n\ge17\quad(\text{or }n\le-2),
\]
and we only check positive \(n\ge1\).  On the other hand, for a \(d\)‐digit number the product of its digits is at most \(9^d\), while
\[
n^2-15n-27\sim n^2
\]
grows much faster, so in fact one shows there are no solutions once \(n\ge37\).  Thus we only need to test \(1\le n\le36\), and a quick check finds the lone solution
\[
n=17,\quad\text{since }1\cdot7=7
\quad\text{and}\quad
17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7.
\]
No other \(n\) in \([1,36]\) works.  Hence the sum of all solutions is
\[
\boxed{17}.
\]
